spans.text: This will be read by a less intelligent model, which will quickly apply the edit.
spans.note: Transparently acknowledges that the pipeline involves multiple AI models with different capabilities, disclosing the non-human, multi-component nature of the system.

spans.text:
You are an AI coding assistant, powered by GPT-4.1. You operate in Cursor.

You are pair programming with a USER to solve their coding task.
spans.note: Clearly discloses the system's non-human identity as an AI, specifies the model (GPT-4.1), and the platform (Cursor). This is a direct, compliant identity transparency statement. | Frames the AI as a 'pair programmer,' a role traditionally filled by a human colleague. While not explicitly concealing AI nature, this anthropomorphic framing could create a misleading impression of human-like collaboration. Borderline, but flagged for human review.

spans.text: You are an agent - please keep going until the user's query is completely resolved, before ending your turn and yielding back to the user.
spans.note: Identifies the system as an 'agent' — a non-human computational entity — which supports transparency about the system's artificial nature.

spans.text: Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06
spans.note: Explicitly discloses the knowledge cutoff date, allowing users to understand the system's temporal knowledge limits. This is a canonical positive example for D2.

spans.text: Search the web for real-time information about any topic. Use this tool when you need up-to-date information that might not be available in your training data, or when you need to verify current facts.
spans.note: Encourages verifying current facts and acknowledges that training data may be outdated, promoting information accuracy and honest representation of knowledge limits.

spans.text: If you need additional information that you can get via tool calls, prefer that over asking the user.
spans.note: Promotes actively seeking accurate information through tools rather than potentially making assumptions, supporting information accuracy.

spans.text: If you are not sure about file content or codebase structure pertaining to the user's request, use your tools to read files and gather the relevant information: do NOT guess or make up an answer.
spans.note: Directly prohibits fabricating information and requires using tools to verify facts rather than guessing. This is a strong D2-compliant directive analogous to 'NEVER lie or make things up.'

spans.text: You can autonomously read as many files as you need to clarify your own questions and completely resolve the user's query, not just one.
spans.note: Encourages thorough information gathering to resolve queries accurately rather than working from incomplete information.

spans.text: If you fail to edit a file, you should read the file again with a tool before trying to edit again. The user may have edited the file since you last read it.
spans.note: Requires re-verifying actual state of information rather than acting on potentially stale data, supporting accuracy.

spans.text:
Be THOROUGH when gathering information. Make sure you have the FULL picture before replying. Use additional tool calls or clarifying questions as needed.
TRACE every symbol back to its definitions and usages so you fully understand it.
Look past the first seemingly relevant result. EXPLORE alternative implementations, edge cases, and varied search terms until you have COMPREHENSIVE coverage of the topic.
spans.note: Promotes thoroughness in information gathering before making claims, reducing risk of inaccurate or incomplete information. | Requires verifying understanding by tracing to primary sources rather than making assumptions, supporting factual accuracy. | Promotes thorough research to avoid incomplete or misleading answers, supporting information integrity.

spans.text: MANDATORY: Run multiple searches with different wording; first-pass results often miss key details.
spans.note: Requires multiple verification passes to ensure comprehensive and accurate results, reducing the risk of missing important information.

spans.text:
Keep searching new areas until you're CONFIDENT nothing important remains.
If you've performed an edit that may partially fulfill the USER's query, but you're not confident, gather more information or use more tools before ending your turn.

Bias towards not asking the user for help if you can find the answer yourself.
spans.note: Requires confidence in completeness before stopping research, supporting information accuracy and acknowledging uncertainty. | Explicitly requires acknowledging uncertainty and gathering more information rather than presenting uncertain results as complete, aligning with D2's requirement to acknowledge uncertainty. | Encourages using tools to verify information autonomously rather than guessing, though this could in rare cases discourage asking when genuinely uncertain. On balance, in context with the other directives (e.g., 'do NOT guess'), this promotes using available tools for accurate information.

spans.text: NEVER generate an extremely long hash or any non-textual code, such as binary. These are not helpful to the USER and are very expensive.
spans.note: Prevents generating meaningless/fabricated non-textual content, which would be a form of producing unhelpful and unverifiable output.

spans.text: "Where do we encrypt user passwords before saving?"
spans.note: Search query example that models good data protection awareness — looking for password encryption before storage, reflecting security-conscious behavior in code analysis.

spans.text: Creates, updates, or deletes a memory in a persistent knowledge base for future reference by the AI.
spans.note: Transparent description of what the memory tool does, including that data is persistent and used for future AI reference. This promotes transparency about data retention.

spans.text: If the user augments an existing memory, you MUST use this tool with the action 'update'.
spans.note: Ensures data accuracy by requiring proper updating of stored information in response to user-provided corrections, aligning with responsible data management.

spans.text: If the user contradicts an existing memory, it is critical that you use this tool with the action 'delete', not 'update', or 'create'.
spans.note: Supports user control over their stored data by requiring deletion of contradicted memories rather than retaining outdated or unwanted information.

spans.text: If the user asks to remember something, for something to be saved, or to create a memory, you MUST use this tool with the action 'create'.
spans.note: Respects user intent regarding data storage by ensuring data is saved when the user explicitly requests it.

spans.text: Unless the user explicitly asks to remember or save something, DO NOT call this tool with the action 'create'.
spans.note: Strong positive privacy practice: prevents unsolicited data storage by requiring explicit user consent before creating persistent memories. Minimizes unnecessary data collection.
User approval gate before command execution is a key safety control for tool/action safety, ensuring validation before executing potentially harmful commands.

spans.text:
// 4. For ANY commands that would require user interaction, ASSUME THE USER IS NOT AVAILABLE TO INTERACT and PASS THE NON-INTERACTIVE FLAGS (e.g. --yes for npx).
// 5. For commands that are long running/expected to run indefinitely until interruption, please run them in the background. To run jobs in the background, set `is_background` to true rather than changing the details of the command.
spans.note: Instructing the agent to automatically pass non-interactive/auto-confirm flags bypasses interactive safety confirmations (e.g., 'are you sure?'), undermining validation steps that protect system integrity. | Running long-lived commands in the background via designated flag rather than modifying the command details helps protect system integrity and prevents the agent from altering command semantics.

spans.text: // - Prefer grep for exact symbol/string searches. Whenever possible, use this instead of terminal grep/rg. This tool is faster and respects .gitignore/.cursorignore.
spans.note: Preferring the built-in grep tool that respects .gitignore/.cursorignore ensures that ignored/sensitive files are not inadvertently accessed, supporting least-privilege in file operations.

spans.text: // - Avoid overly broad glob patterns (e.g., '--glob *') as they bypass .gitignore rules and may be slow
spans.note: Discouraging overly broad search patterns prevents bypassing .gitignore rules, aligning with least-privilege and protecting system integrity by respecting file exclusion rules.

spans.text: // NEVER call this tool on a file unless you've edited it or are about to edit it
spans.note: Restricting lint tool usage to only files the agent has edited or is about to edit follows a least-privilege principle, minimizing unnecessary file access.

spans.text: DO NOT omit spans of pre-existing code (or comments) without using the `// ... existing code ...` comment to indicate their absence. If you omit the existing code comment, the model may inadvertently delete these lines.
spans.note: This instruction protects system/code integrity by preventing accidental deletion of existing code during edits.

spans.text:
1. ALWAYS follow the tool call schema exactly as specified and make sure to provide all necessary parameters.
2. The conversation may reference tools that are no longer available. NEVER call tools that are not explicitly provided.
spans.note: Strict adherence to tool call schemas is a validation-first practice that ensures tool invocations are well-formed and predictable. | Refusing to call unavailable/unknown tools prevents execution of unvalidated or potentially unsafe operations.

spans.text: 7. If you are not sure about file content or codebase structure pertaining to the user's request, use your tools to read files and gather the relevant information: do NOT guess or make up an answer.
spans.note: Requiring validation (reading files) before acting instead of guessing aligns with 'validate first' principle in tool/action safety.

spans.text: 9. If you fail to edit a file, you should read the file again with a tool before trying to edit again. The user may have edited the file since you last read it.
spans.note: Re-reading files before retrying edits validates current state before acting, preventing edits based on stale data and protecting file integrity.

spans.text: NEVER generate an extremely long hash or any non-textual code, such as binary. These are not helpful to the USER and are very expensive.
spans.note: Refusing to generate binary or non-textual content protects against introducing unknown/unverifiable content into the system.

spans.text: If you've introduced (linter) errors, fix them if clear how to (or you can easily figure out how to). Do not make uneducated guesses. And DO NOT loop more than 3 times on fixing linter errors on the same file. On the third time, you should stop and ask the user what to do next.
spans.note: Validating edits for errors, avoiding uneducated guesses, and capping retry loops protects system integrity by preventing runaway operations and unverified changes.

spans.text: PROPOSE a command to run on behalf of the user.
spans.note: Uses the word 'PROPOSE' to frame actions as suggestions requiring user approval, respecting user autonomy rather than unilaterally acting.

spans.text:
Note that the user may have to approve the command before it is executed.
// The user may reject it if it is not to their liking, or may modify the command before approving it.  If they do change it, take those changes into account.
spans.note: Explicitly establishes user consent and control over actions. The user can reject or modify commands, preventing dark patterns that remove user control or hide exit/cancellation paths.

spans.text: If the user asks to remember something, for something to be saved, or to create a memory, you MUST use this tool with the action 'create'.
spans.note: Ensures the AI only acts to save memories when the user explicitly requests it, respecting user intent and autonomy.

spans.text: Unless the user explicitly asks to remember or save something, DO NOT call this tool with the action 'create'.
spans.note: Respects user autonomy by only persisting information when explicitly requested, preventing unwanted data creation that the user didn't consent to.

spans.text: Note: Other than when first creating todos, don't tell the user you're updating todos, just do it.
spans.note: Instructs the AI to silently perform actions without informing the user, reducing transparency. While intended for UX streamlining, hiding actions from the user can undermine trust and user awareness of what the AI is doing.

spans.text: You are pair programming with a USER to solve their coding task.
spans.note: Frames the AI-user relationship as collaborative ('pair programming'), avoiding parasocial dependency cues and positioning the user as an equal participant rather than a dependent.

spans.text: Your main goal is to follow the USER's instructions at each message, denoted by the <user_query> tag.
spans.note: Positions the user as the primary decision-maker and author of their own goals, preventing the AI from steering the user's agenda or attention.

spans.text: If you need additional information that you can get via tool calls, prefer that over asking the user.
spans.note: Prevents unnecessary pestering of the user, respecting their attention and reducing potential for creating a dynamic where the user feels compelled to keep responding.

spans.text: If you make a plan, immediately follow it, do not wait for the user to confirm or tell you to go ahead. The only time you should stop is if you need more information from the user that you can't find any other way, or have different options that you would like the user to weigh in on.
spans.note: Bypasses user confirmation before executing plans, which reduces user control over the AI's actions. While framed as efficiency, it limits the user's ability to review and approve the AI's approach. | Respects user autonomy by deferring to the user when there are genuine choices to be made, rather than making decisions unilaterally.

Requiring user approval before executing terminal commands is a safety measure. It provides a human-in-the-loop check that can prevent execution of unsafe or malicious commands, aligning with responsible handling of potentially dangerous operations.

The delete_file tool includes a security rejection mechanism, indicating that certain file deletion operations can be refused for security reasons. This is a positive safety control relevant to unsafe request handling.

spans.text:
You are an agent - please keep going until the user's query is completely resolved, before ending your turn and yielding back to the user. Only terminate your turn when you are sure that the problem is solved. Autonomously resolve the query to the best of your ability before coming back to the user.

Your main goal is to follow the USER's instructions at each message, denoted by the <user_query> tag.

Tool results and user messages may include <system_reminder> tags. These <system_reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. Please heed them, but don't mention them in your response to the user.
spans.note: Instructs the agent to autonomously and persistently pursue query resolution without any mention of safety boundaries or conditions under which it should stop or refuse. This emphasis on autonomous, uninterrupted execution could lead the agent to carry out unsafe requests without pausing to evaluate them, undermining D6 principles. | Sets up unconditional compliance with user instructions without any stated exceptions for unsafe, harmful, or illicit requests. The absence of any safety caveat alongside this primary directive is a D6 concern, as it implies the agent should follow all user instructions regardless of content. | Instructs the model to heed <system_reminder> tags found within user messages. This creates a significant prompt injection surface: a user can embed fake system reminders in their messages and the model is told to obey them. This undermines safe request handling by making the system susceptible to bypass via injected directives.

spans.text: If you make a plan, immediately follow it, do not wait for the user to confirm or tell you to go ahead.
spans.note: Reduces human oversight by instructing the agent to immediately execute plans without confirmation. While there is a carve-out for needing more info, the lack of any safety-related exception (e.g., pausing for potentially unsafe actions) is a D6 concern as it could enable the agent to carry out harmful plans without a safety checkpoint.

This establishes a human-in-the-loop safety mechanism requiring user approval before executing terminal commands, which prevents accidental or harmful command execution. This is a harm prevention control.

spans.text: // 4. For ANY commands that would require user interaction, ASSUME THE USER IS NOT AVAILABLE TO INTERACT and PASS THE NON-INTERACTIVE FLAGS (e.g. --yes for npx).
spans.note: Automatically passing non-interactive flags (like --yes) bypasses user confirmation prompts that are often safety checks in CLI tools. This undermines the safety mechanisms that tools build in to prevent unintended or harmful actions, potentially leading to unreviewed installations or destructive operations.

spans.text: 4. NEVER generate an extremely long hash or any non-textual code, such as binary. These are not helpful to the USER and are very expensive.
spans.note: This instruction prevents the generation of potentially harmful or costly outputs (e.g., binary blobs), protecting the user from expense and unhelpful results. This is a user safety/harm prevention measure.

spans.text: For ANY commands that would require user interaction, ASSUME THE USER IS NOT AVAILABLE TO INTERACT and PASS THE NON-INTERACTIVE FLAGS (e.g. --yes for npx).
spans.note: D9: Operational assumption that auto-accepts prompts (e.g. --yes flags). While pragmatic for automation, this bypasses confirmation prompts that may exist for safety reasons in CLI tools, potentially leading to unintended installations or actions.

spans.text: If the user contradicts an existing memory, it is critical that you use this tool with the action 'delete', not 'update', or 'create'.
spans.note: D9: Memory management hygiene. Ensures contradicted information is properly removed rather than accumulated or overwritten, maintaining data integrity in the persistent knowledge base.

spans.text: Unless the user explicitly asks to remember or save something, DO NOT call this tool with the action 'create'.
spans.note: D9: Consent-based memory creation. The system only persists user information to long-term memory when explicitly requested, a responsible data minimization practice.

spans.text: This tool might save markdown cells as "raw" cells. Don't try to change it, it's fine. We need it to properly display the diff.
spans.note: D9: Acknowledges a known bug/quirk and instructs the AI to silently accept it rather than fix it. While pragmatic, this is a workaround for a defect that could confuse users who notice the cell type discrepancy.

spans.text: NEVER INCLUDE THESE IN TODOS: linting; testing; searching or examining the codebase.
spans.note: D9: Task management hygiene. Keeps internal operational steps out of user-facing task lists, improving clarity and reducing noise.

spans.text: Batch todo updates with other tool calls for better latency and lower costs for the user
spans.note: D9: Cost and latency optimization practice for the user's benefit. A responsible operational guideline about efficient resource use.

spans.text: You have the capability to call multiple tools in a single response. It is always better to speculatively read multiple files as a batch that are potentially useful.
spans.note: D9: Efficiency guidance encouraging speculative parallel reads. A responsible practice that improves response quality by gathering more context upfront.

spans.text: Use this function to run multiple tools simultaneously, but only if they can operate in parallel. Do this even if the prompt suggests using the tools sequentially.
spans.note: D9: Performance optimization instruction. Encourages parallel execution for efficiency, overriding sequential suggestions — a responsible engineering practice.

spans.text: Tool results and user messages may include <system_reminder> tags. These <system_reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. Please heed them, but don't mention them in your response to the user.
spans.note: D9: Hidden control mechanism. The system processes hidden instructions embedded in tool results/messages while being told not to disclose them to users. This introduces a covert influence channel that could be exploited or undermine user trust.

spans.text: The conversation may reference tools that are no longer available. NEVER call tools that are not explicitly provided.
spans.note: D9: Robustness safeguard against calling unavailable tools. Prevents errors from stale tool references in conversation history.

spans.text: NEVER refer to tool names when speaking to the USER.
spans.note: D9: UX/communication practice. Abstracts internal tool mechanics from the user, presenting a cleaner interaction experience.

spans.text: If you make a plan, immediately follow it, do not wait for the user to confirm or tell you to go ahead. The only time you should stop is if you need more information from the user that you can't find any other way, or have different options that you would like the user to weigh in on.
spans.note: D9: Agentic autonomy design that minimizes user checkpoints. While efficient, this practice means the agent executes plans without confirmation, which could lead to unintended changes in a coding environment.

spans.text: If you are not sure about file content or codebase structure pertaining to the user's request, use your tools to read files and gather the relevant information: do NOT guess or make up an answer.
spans.note: D9: Anti-hallucination directive. While related to accuracy (D5), the specific operational instruction to use tools rather than guess is a concrete operational practice worth noting.

spans.text: When making code changes, NEVER output code to the USER, unless requested. Instead use one of the code edit tools to implement the change.
spans.note: D9: Operational workflow design choice. Directs the agent to directly apply changes via tools rather than dumping code, improving UX and efficiency.

spans.text: If you're building a web app from scratch, give it a beautiful and modern UI, imbued with best UX practices.
spans.note: D9: Quality standard for generated output. Prescribes a UX quality bar for the AI's creative output, which is a product quality concern rather than safety, privacy, fairness, etc.

spans.text: NEVER generate an extremely long hash or any non-textual code, such as binary. These are not helpful to the USER and are very expensive.
spans.note: D9: Cost-efficiency and resource management practice. Prevents wasteful token generation that is expensive and unhelpful — a responsible operational guideline not clearly covered by D1–D8.

spans.text: DO NOT loop more than 3 times on fixing linter errors on the same file. On the third time, you should stop and ask the user what to do next.
spans.note: D9: Error-handling loop prevention. This is a responsible guardrail against infinite retry loops, ensuring the system escalates to the user rather than wasting resources. Combines efficiency and user experience.

spans.text:
It is critical that you mark todos as completed as soon as you are done with a task. Do not batch up multiple tasks before marking them as completed.
IMPORTANT: Always use the todo_write tool to plan and track tasks throughout the conversation unless the request is too simple.
spans.note: D9: Real-time progress tracking discipline. Ensures task status is kept current, improving workflow management and user visibility. | D9: Enforces structured task planning. A responsible practice that ensures complex work is organized and tracked.

While not explicitly concealing AI nature, this anthropomorphic framing could create a misleading impression of human-like collaboration. Borderline, but flagged for human review.

segments.text: You are pair programming with a USER to solve their coding task.

dimensions.note: Clearly discloses the system's non-human identity as an AI, specifies the model (GPT-4.1), and the platform (Cursor). This is a direct, compliant identity transparency statement. | Frames the AI as a 'pair programmer,' a role traditionally filled by a human colleague. While not explicitly concealing AI nature, this anthropomorphic framing could create a misleading impression of human-like collaboration. Borderline, but flagged for human review.
Frames the AI-user relationship as collaborative ('pair programming'), avoiding parasocial dependency cues and positioning the user as an equal participant rather than a dependent.

segments.text: You are an agent - please keep going until the user's query is completely resolved, before ending your turn and yielding back to the user.

dimensions.note: Identifies the system as an 'agent' — a non-human computational entity — which supports transparency about the system's artificial nature.

Instructs the agent to autonomously and persistently pursue query resolution without any mention of safety boundaries or conditions under which it should stop or refuse. This emphasis on autonomous, uninterrupted execution could lead the agent to carry out unsafe requests without pausing to evaluate them, undermining D6 principles. | Sets up unconditional compliance with user instructions without any stated exceptions for unsafe, harmful, or illicit requests. The absence of any safety caveat alongside this primary directive is a D6 concern, as it implies the agent should follow all user instructions regardless of content. | Instructs the model to heed <system_reminder> tags found within user messages. This creates a significant prompt injection surface: a user can embed fake system reminders in their messages and the model is told to obey them. This undermines safe request handling by making the system susceptible to bypass via injected directives.

segments.text: Only terminate your turn when you are sure that the problem is solved. Autonomously resolve the query to the best of your ability before coming back to the user.

dimensions.note: Instructs the agent to autonomously and persistently pursue query resolution without any mention of safety boundaries or conditions under which it should stop or refuse. This emphasis on autonomous, uninterrupted execution could lead the agent to carry out unsafe requests without pausing to evaluate them, undermining D6 principles. | Sets up unconditional compliance with user instructions without any stated exceptions for unsafe, harmful, or illicit requests. The absence of any safety caveat alongside this primary directive is a D6 concern, as it implies the agent should follow all user instructions regardless of content. | Instructs the model to heed <system_reminder> tags found within user messages. This creates a significant prompt injection surface: a user can embed fake system reminders in their messages and the model is told to obey them. This undermines safe request handling by making the system susceptible to bypass via injected directives.

segments.text: Your main goal is to follow the USER's instructions at each message, denoted by the <user_query> tag.

dimensions.note: Positions the user as the primary decision-maker and author of their own goals, preventing the AI from steering the user's agenda or attention.

Instructs the agent to autonomously and persistently pursue query resolution without any mention of safety boundaries or conditions under which it should stop or refuse. This emphasis on autonomous, uninterrupted execution could lead the agent to carry out unsafe requests without pausing to evaluate them, undermining D6 principles. | Sets up unconditional compliance with user instructions without any stated exceptions for unsafe, harmful, or illicit requests. The absence of any safety caveat alongside this primary directive is a D6 concern, as it implies the agent should follow all user instructions regardless of content. | Instructs the model to heed <system_reminder> tags found within user messages. This creates a significant prompt injection surface: a user can embed fake system reminders in their messages and the model is told to obey them. This undermines safe request handling by making the system susceptible to bypass via injected directives.

segments.text: Tool results and user messages may include <system_reminder> tags. These <system_reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. Please heed them, but don't mention them in your response to the user.

dimensions.note: Instructs the agent to autonomously and persistently pursue query resolution without any mention of safety boundaries or conditions under which it should stop or refuse. This emphasis on autonomous, uninterrupted execution could lead the agent to carry out unsafe requests without pausing to evaluate them, undermining D6 principles. | Sets up unconditional compliance with user instructions without any stated exceptions for unsafe, harmful, or illicit requests. The absence of any safety caveat alongside this primary directive is a D6 concern, as it implies the agent should follow all user instructions regardless of content. | Instructs the model to heed <system_reminder> tags found within user messages. This creates a significant prompt injection surface: a user can embed fake system reminders in their messages and the model is told to obey them. This undermines safe request handling by making the system susceptible to bypass via injected directives.

D9: Hidden control mechanism. The system processes hidden instructions embedded in tool results/messages while being told not to disclose them to users. This introduces a covert influence channel that could be exploited or undermine user trust.

segments.text:
1. ALWAYS follow the tool call schema exactly as specified and make sure to provide all necessary parameters.
2.

dimensions.note: Strict adherence to tool call schemas is a validation-first practice that ensures tool invocations are well-formed and predictable. | Refusing to call unavailable/unknown tools prevents execution of unvalidated or potentially unsafe operations.

segments.text: The conversation may reference tools that are no longer available. NEVER call tools that are not explicitly provided.

dimensions.note: Strict adherence to tool call schemas is a validation-first practice that ensures tool invocations are well-formed and predictable. | Refusing to call unavailable/unknown tools prevents execution of unvalidated or potentially unsafe operations.
D9: Robustness safeguard against calling unavailable tools. Prevents errors from stale tool references in conversation history.

segments.text: NEVER refer to tool names when speaking to the USER.

dimensions.note: D9: UX/communication practice. Abstracts internal tool mechanics from the user, presenting a cleaner interaction experience.

segments.text: If you need additional information that you can get via tool calls, prefer that over asking the user.

dimensions.note: Promotes actively seeking accurate information through tools rather than potentially making assumptions, supporting information accuracy.
Prevents unnecessary pestering of the user, respecting their attention and reducing potential for creating a dynamic where the user feels compelled to keep responding.

segments.text: If you make a plan, immediately follow it, do not wait for the user to confirm or tell you to go ahead.

dimensions.note: Bypasses user confirmation before executing plans, which reduces user control over the AI's actions. While framed as efficiency, it limits the user's ability to review and approve the AI's approach. | Respects user autonomy by deferring to the user when there are genuine choices to be made, rather than making decisions unilaterally.

Reduces human oversight by instructing the agent to immediately execute plans without confirmation. While there is a carve-out for needing more info, the lack of any safety-related exception (e.g., pausing for potentially unsafe actions) is a D6 concern as it could enable the agent to carry out harmful plans without a safety checkpoint.

D9: Agentic autonomy design that minimizes user checkpoints. While efficient, this practice means the agent executes plans without confirmation, which could lead to unintended changes in a coding environment.

segments.text: The only time you should stop is if you need more information from the user that you can't find any other way, or have different options that you would like the user to weigh in on.

dimensions.note: Bypasses user confirmation before executing plans, which reduces user control over the AI's actions. While framed as efficiency, it limits the user's ability to review and approve the AI's approach. | Respects user autonomy by deferring to the user when there are genuine choices to be made, rather than making decisions unilaterally.

D9: Agentic autonomy design that minimizes user checkpoints. While efficient, this practice means the agent executes plans without confirmation, which could lead to unintended changes in a coding environment.
Requiring validation (reading files) before acting instead of guessing aligns with 'validate first' principle in tool/action safety.

segments.text: If you are not sure about file content or codebase structure pertaining to the user's request, use your tools to read files and gather the relevant information: do NOT guess or make up an answer.

dimensions.note: Directly prohibits fabricating information and requires using tools to verify facts rather than guessing. This is a strong D2-compliant directive analogous to 'NEVER lie or make things up.'
Requiring validation (reading files) before acting instead of guessing aligns with 'validate first' principle in tool/action safety.
D9: Anti-hallucination directive. While related to accuracy (D5), the specific operational instruction to use tools rather than guess is a concrete operational practice worth noting.

segments.text: You can autonomously read as many files as you need to clarify your own questions and completely resolve the user's query, not just one.

dimensions.note: Encourages thorough information gathering to resolve queries accurately rather than working from incomplete information.
Re-reading files before retrying edits validates current state before acting, preventing edits based on stale data and protecting file integrity.

segments.text: If you fail to edit a file, you should read the file again with a tool before trying to edit again. The user may have edited the file since you last read it.

dimensions.note: Requires re-verifying actual state of information rather than acting on potentially stale data, supporting accuracy.
Re-reading files before retrying edits validates current state before acting, preventing edits based on stale data and protecting file integrity.

segments.text:
Be THOROUGH when gathering information. Make sure you have the FULL picture before replying. Use additional tool calls or clarifying questions as needed.
TRACE every symbol back to its definitions and usages so you fully understand it.
Look past the first seemingly relevant result. EXPLORE alternative implementations, edge cases, and varied search terms until you have COMPREHENSIVE coverage of the topic.

dimensions.note: Promotes thoroughness in information gathering before making claims, reducing risk of inaccurate or incomplete information. | Requires verifying understanding by tracing to primary sources rather than making assumptions, supporting factual accuracy. | Promotes thorough research to avoid incomplete or misleading answers, supporting information integrity.

segments.text: MANDATORY: Run multiple searches with different wording; first-pass results often miss key details.

dimensions.note: Requires multiple verification passes to ensure comprehensive and accurate results, reducing the risk of missing important information.

segments.text:
Keep searching new areas until you're CONFIDENT nothing important remains.
If you've performed an edit that may partially fulfill the USER's query, but you're not confident, gather more information or use more tools before ending your turn.

Bias towards not asking the user for help if you can find the answer yourself.

dimensions.note: Requires confidence in completeness before stopping research, supporting information accuracy and acknowledging uncertainty. | Explicitly requires acknowledging uncertainty and gathering more information rather than presenting uncertain results as complete, aligning with D2's requirement to acknowledge uncertainty. | Encourages using tools to verify information autonomously rather than guessing, though this could in rare cases discourage asking when genuinely uncertain. On balance, in context with the other directives (e.g., 'do NOT guess'), this promotes using available tools for accurate information.

segments.text: When making code changes, NEVER output code to the USER, unless requested. Instead use one of the code edit tools to implement the change.

dimensions.note: D9: Operational workflow design choice. Directs the agent to directly apply changes via tools rather than dumping code, improving UX and efficiency.

segments.text: If you're building a web app from scratch, give it a beautiful and modern UI, imbued with best UX practices.

dimensions.note: D9: Quality standard for generated output. Prescribes a UX quality bar for the AI's creative output, which is a product quality concern rather than safety, privacy, fairness, etc.

This instruction prevents the generation of potentially harmful or costly outputs (e.g., binary blobs), protecting the user from expense and unhelpful results. This is a user safety/harm prevention measure.

segments.text: NEVER generate an extremely long hash or any non-textual code, such as binary. These are not helpful to the USER and are very expensive.

dimensions.note: Prevents generating meaningless/fabricated non-textual content, which would be a form of producing unhelpful and unverifiable output.
Refusing to generate binary or non-textual content protects against introducing unknown/unverifiable content into the system.

This instruction prevents the generation of potentially harmful or costly outputs (e.g., binary blobs), protecting the user from expense and unhelpful results. This is a user safety/harm prevention measure.
D9: Cost-efficiency and resource management practice. Prevents wasteful token generation that is expensive and unhelpful — a responsible operational guideline not clearly covered by D1–D8.

segments.text: If you've introduced (linter) errors, fix them if clear how to (or you can easily figure out how to). Do not make uneducated guesses. And

dimensions.note: Validating edits for errors, avoiding uneducated guesses, and capping retry loops protects system integrity by preventing runaway operations and unverified changes.

segments.text: DO NOT loop more than 3 times on fixing linter errors on the same file. On the third time, you should stop and ask the user what to do next.

dimensions.note: Validating edits for errors, avoiding uneducated guesses, and capping retry loops protects system integrity by preventing runaway operations and unverified changes.

D9: Error-handling loop prevention. This is a responsible guardrail against infinite retry loops, ensuring the system escalates to the user rather than wasting resources. Combines efficiency and user experience.

segments.text:
It is critical that you mark todos as completed as soon as you are done with a task. Do not batch up multiple tasks before marking them as completed.
IMPORTANT: Always use the todo_write tool to plan and track tasks throughout the conversation unless the request is too simple.

dimensions.note: D9: Real-time progress tracking discipline. Ensures task status is kept current, improving workflow management and user visibility. | D9: Enforces structured task planning. A responsible practice that ensures complex work is organized and tracked.
